title: How Dinosaur Skeletons Became So Expensive
gist: Sotheby's sold the T. rex skeleton "Gus" for over $50 million, extending a boom in private dinosaur-fossil collecting made possible because, unlike Brazil or Mongolia, U.S. law lets landowners rather than the state or discovering paleontologists own and sell fossils found on their land.

insights.claim: Sotheby's auctioned the 38-foot T. rex skeleton "Gus" for over $50 million, setting a new high for a dinosaur fossil sale to a private collector.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Marks a new price ceiling in the booming market for museum-grade fossils sold to private buyers.
insights.locator: The going rate is now over $50 million

insights.claim: A growing trend of wealthy collectors buying dinosaur skeletons at auction is drawing criticism from paleontologists.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Signals a widening gap between the scientific community and a market willing to pay record sums for fossils.
insights.locator: It's part of a growing trend

insights.claim: In the United States, dinosaur fossils found on private land legally belong to the landowner, unlike in Brazil or Mongolia, where fossils are designated state property.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: This property-rights asymmetry is the structural reason the U.S. is a primary source of fossils feeding the private auction market.
insights.locator: Unlike countries such as Brazil and Mongolia

insights.claim: Commercial fossil-hunting outfits like Theropoda Expeditions strike profit-sharing deals with private landowners to excavate valuable fossils, a structure that cuts professional paleontologists out of the proceeds.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Explains the commercial supply chain feeding record-breaking fossil auctions, and why scientists have little financial stake in it.
